coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing of peroxide explosives based on inner filter effect with boosted sensitivity and selectivity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: CAO, Hui-Yu
CAI, Zhen-Zhen
LI, Yu-Shu
WANG, Guang-Fa
DOU, Xin-Cun
coredata.dc:description: As one of the most popular improvised explosives, the detection of peroxide-based explosives (PBEs) is of great urgency for reducing or potentially stemming explosive-related terrorist attacks. However, few of the currently developed optical sensor focus on the direct detection of PBEs. Here, a general design strategy for the direct, sensitive and specific coloerimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing of peroxides was proposed by triggering the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) in the carbon dots/titanyl sulfate (CDs/TiOSO4) sensing system. The colorimetric and fluorescent limits of detection (LODs) of the proposed CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system towards hydrogen peroxide as representative were calculated to be 31.3 μM and 0.2 μM, respectively. Further practical sensing performances of the sensing system with incorperation of hydrogel platform demonstrated the universality for the detection of various PBEs in liquid, solid and even vapor phases with a LOD of 0.15 ppb for vapor sensing. This study provides an inspirable strategy for designing IFE-based dual-mode sensors for on-site visual detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peroxides
IFE
Colorimetric
Fluorescent
Dual-mode Sensing

Preparation of the Hydrogel-based sensing platform
Design of the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system
CDs for IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing
Mechanism establishment of the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system
Hydrogen peroxide sensing performance of the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system
The IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing platform for peroxide-based explosives

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system design.

Characterization of the structure and optical properties of the CDs: (a) TEM images of the obtained CDs; (b) corresponding size distribution of the CDs; (c) XRD patterns of the CDs; (d) FTIR spectrum of the CDs; (e) UV-vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of the CDs; (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs with excitation of different wavelength.

The IFE mechanism establishment of the CDs/TiOSO4 colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system: (a) the fluorescence spectra and the corresponding fluorescent photographs of the system before and after addition of hydrogen peroxide; (b) The fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs and absorption spectra of TiOSO4 and Ti(O2)2+•H2O; (c) the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs after adding different amounts of the colorimetric reaction product Ti(O2)2+•H2O; (d) The fluorescence lifetime spectra of the proposed sensing system before and after addition of hydrogen peroxide.

The sensitivity of CDs/TiOSO4 IFE-based dual-mode system towards hydrogen peroxide: (a) fluorescence emission spectra and the corresponding fluorescence photographs with addition of different concentration of hydrogen peroxide; (b) Emission intensity at 457 nm as a function of hydrogen peroxide concentration; (c) UV-vis absorption spectra and the corresponding photograph with addition of different concentration of hydrogen peroxide; (d) Absorption intensity at 417 nm as a function of hydrogen peroxide concentration.

The selectivity of CDs/TiOSO4 IFE-based dual-mode system towards hydrogen peroxide: (a) Fluorescence emission spectra after adding same amount of different substances (1 mM); b) Histogram of fluorescence quenching efficiency at 457 nm after adding same amount of different substances; c) Absorption spectra after adding same amount of different substances (1 mM); d) The absorption intensity at 457 nm after adding same amount of different substances.

The sensing application of CDs/TiOSO4 IFE-based dual-mode system towards peroxide-based explosives: (a) The colorimetric and fluorescent photographs of TiOSO4/CDs system before and after sensing of different kinds of peroxides; (b) Schematic illustration of the sensing principle of the CDs/TiOSO4 IFE-based dual-mode hydrogel sensing platform; (c) B values of the hydrogel sensor as the function of hydrogen peroxide concentration added; (d) Relative fluorescence emission intensity values of the dried hydrogel sensor as a function of hydrogen peroxide vapor concentration.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing of peroxide explosives based on inner filter effect with boosted sensitivity and selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices for Special Environments

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCenter of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics & Chemistry, Urumqi, Xinjiang, 830000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics & Chemistry, Urumqi

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one of the most popular improvised explosives, the detection of peroxide-based explosives (PBEs) is of great urgency for reducing or potentially stemming explosive-related terrorist attacks. However, few of the currently developed optical sensor focus on the direct detection of PBEs. Here, a general design strategy for the direct, sensitive and specific coloerimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing of peroxides was proposed by triggering the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) in the carbon dots/titanyl sulfate (CDs/TiOSO4) sensing system. The colorimetric and fluorescent limits of detection (LODs) of the proposed CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system towards hydrogen peroxide as representative were calculated to be 31.3 μM and 0.2 μM, respectively. Further practical sensing performances of the sensing system with incorperation of hydrogel platform demonstrated the universality for the detection of various PBEs in liquid, solid and even vapor phases with a LOD of 0.15 ppb for vapor sensing. This study provides an inspirable strategy for designing IFE-based dual-mode sensors for on-site visual detection.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, improvised explosives, including peroxide-based explosives (PBEs), have become the prior choice of terrorists and have been used to participate in numerous terrorist attacks [1–14]. As a famous representative of PBEs, triacetone triperoxide (TATP) is one of the most sensitive explosives , which owns 88% explosive equivalence of TNT [15]. The powerful PBEs can be made facilely by simply mixing the commercially available precursors, including H2O2, acetone and alcohol, with the aid of acid [16]. While under certain circumstance, the PBEs will decompose and release H2O2. However, the detection of PBEs is quite difficult due to their lacking of the nitro groups as ordinary explosives [17]. Therefore, it is of great significance to detect trace H2O2 which is not only the precursor but also the decomposition product of PBEs, as the PBE marker for individual health [18], social stability and homeland security [19].

Due to the fact that H2O2 is the major reactive oxygen species and an important oxidation metabolite of life systems [20], a variety of methods have been developed for the detection of H2O2, including mass spectrometry [21], Raman spectrum [22], ion mobility spectrometry [23], electrochemistry, chemiluminescence [24], chemical colorimetry and fluorescence [12,25], and so on. Among them, the visual detection methodology, namely fluorometry and colorimetry fluorometry, has attracted extensive attention owing to the simple operation, good specificity, fast response and ease of portability [26,27]. For the fluorescent detection of H2O2, several small molecule probes have been developed, such as boronate deprotection-based systems [28,29], fluorogenic fluorescein derivatives [30,31] and so on [32–34], which are not environmentally friendly and generally require complicated synthesis.

Compared with the above single mode visual detection methods, the colorimetric-florescent dual-mode method is superior owning to the high specificity, sensitivity and its facility for naked eye observation [35–37]. The dual-mode sensing system with IFE mechanism are well known with merits of simple fabrication, facile modification, strong universality [38]. The dual-mode sensing of H2O2 has been realized by combining the enzyme-catalyzed colorimetric reaction with peroxidase like horseradish peroxidase (HRP) or mimic enzyme and coloring substrate like o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and the appropriate fluorophores [39,40], which limits the selectivity from other strong oxidants.

Among the colorimetric detection methods, the Titanium(IV) reagent is one of the most selective methods due to the fact that it is based on the chelating reaction between the Titanium(IV) moiety and the peroxide moiety [41], which directly generates the color products. For instance, the colorless Titanium(IV) sulfate solution reacting with peroxide will generate the three-membered ring structure of Ti(O2)2+ with maximum absorption wavelength of around 410 nm, leading to the color change to yellow [42,43]. Furthermore, the color of the product could change to purple upon the chemical environment of Titanium(IV) change with specific ligands [44]. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of nanomaterial, is increasingly attractive in the field of fluorescent sensing owing to their full-wavelength adjustable optical properties, good tolerance and easy functionalization [45,46]. Therefore, it is attracting to fabricate a colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode IFE-based system for peroxides sensing by combining the advantage of both sides for high sensitivity and good specificity.

Herein, a facile CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system has been developed for colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing of peroxide-based explosives based on IFE with comprehensively boosted sensitivity and selectivity. With the addition of hydrogen peroxide, as a representative of PBEs, TiOSO4 in the system formed the specific yellow-colored (TiO2)2+ product, which quenched the blue emission of the CDs in the system by the well overlap of absorption spectra of the product centered at 417 nm and the emission spectra centered at 457 nm based on the IFE mechanism. More importantly, with incorporation of polyacrylamide (PAM) hydrogel substrate, the CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system was applied for the on-site colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode visual detection of peroxides, including peroxide-based explosives, in liquid, solid and even vapor phases.

All chemicals were in analytical-reagent grade and used as received. Titanyl sulfate (TiOSO4) was obtained from Aldrich. Citric acid (CA) and sulphur (S) was obtained from Tianjin Baishi Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium peroxide (Na2O2), calcium peroxide (CaO2) diethylenetriamine (DETA), dichloromethane (DCM), N,N'-methylene-bis-acrylamide, ammonium persulphate ((NH4)2S2O8), acrylamide (AM), N,N,N',N'-tetramethylethylenediamine, sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai China). Urea (CH4N2O), ammonium nitrate ((NH4)2CO3), sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3), sodium bisulfite (NaHSO3), potassium bisulfate (KHSO4), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), potassium iodide (KI), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), potassium nitrate (KNO3), potassium chlorate (KCl), sodium perchlorate (NaClO4), and ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium bromide (NaBr), and sodium sulphate (Na2SO4) were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation. 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), para-nitrotoluene (PNT), triacetone triperoxide (TATP), and hexa-methylene trioxide diamine (HMTD) were obtained from security department. Ultrapure deionized water (18.2 MΩ) was used to prepare solutions.

The X-ray powder diffraction data were collected on a Bruker AXS D8-Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation of λ = 1.5418 Å at 40 kV and 40 mA (scanning from 2θ = 5 to 80° with step of 0.01° and speed of 1° min−1). UV-vis spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U3900 UV-vis spectrophotometer. Emission spectra and efficiency were obtained on an Edinburgh FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer. Time-resolved photoluminescence decay measurements were carried out using a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-7610F Plus, 4.0-6.0 kV) was used to characterize the morphology of the samples. Elemental analysis was performed by an energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, OXFORD X-Max 50) equipped on the FE-SEM. Attenuated total reflection fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra were obtained using a PerkinElmer Frontier with a universal ATR sampling accessory from PerkinElmer. The optical digital photographs were captured by a Vision Datum Mars 5000S-20gc industrial camera.

CA and DETA were chosen as carbon source and nitrogen source respectively to prepare CDs. 0.7 g CA was dissolved in 16.7 mL distilled water and 1.5 mL DETA was then added into the solution under stirring. Then the solution was transferred to 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave, sealed and heated at 200 °C for 8 h in the oven. After cooling down to the room temperature naturally, the crude CDs solution was obtained. The crude CDs solution was then dried at 80 °C in the oven and the viscous liquid yielded. The viscous liquid was washed, sonicated with DCM and then evaporated to yield the blue fluorescent solid CDs which was then dispersed in water for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the IFE dual-mode sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, the IFE dual-mode sensing system was constructed by mixing 1 g/L CDs with the mixture solution of TiOSO4 and concentrated sulfuric acid (volume ratio 1:1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Hydrogel-based sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The PAM hydrogel was prepared using following procedure. 1.4 g acrylamide and 4 mg N,N,N',N'-tetramethylethylenediamine were dispersed in 10 mL distilled water with assistant of sonication. Next, 5 μL N,N'-methylene-bis-acrylamide, 2 mg ammonium persulphate wer added into the solution under stirring, respectively. The solution was added into handmade glass mode and kept heating at 50 °C for 2 h for completed polymerization. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained PAM hydrogel was removed from the mode and then immersed in DI water for 48 h by replacing water every 6 h for thoroughly removal of the unreacted reagents for use.

The as-prepared PAM hydrogel was cut in round shape with diameter of 10 mm and rectangle shape (17 × 10 mm) and then immersed in 100 mL TiOSO4/CDs solution for 24 h at room temperature to yield the TiOSO4/CDs incorporated hydrogel, namely the hydrogel-based IFE dual-mode sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system is designed based on the highly selective colorimetric method of Titanium(IV) reagent in order to utilize its specific complexing reactivity with peroxide. As shown in Fig. 1 , the IFE-based sensing system for peroxide could be constructed by combining the Titanium(IV) reagent, which was acidic titanyl sulfate for instance, with a proper fluorophore, such as CDs, whose emission spectra overlap barely with the absorption spectra of the Titanium(IV) reagent and overlap well with that of the Ti(O2)2+ product to trigger the IFE phenomena for detecting peroxides. Therefore, the designed IFE-based sensing system was colorless under sunlight with no absorption peak within the visible region and emitted blue light under UV with emission peak in the range of 400-450 nm. After addition of peroxide, the system showed yellow color under sunlight with characteristic absorption peak of Ti(O2)2+ product at around 410 nm and a quenched fluorescence due to the switch-on of IFE phenomena, revealing the colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing manner of the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs for IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs with emission around 450 nm was designed to overlap with the absorption region of the (TiO2)2+ product in order to construct the IFE-based system for colorimetric-fluorescent sensing of peroxides. The CDs was readily prepared via hydrothermal method by using citric acid and DETA as precursors. As shown in the TEM image and corresponding size distribution analysis (Fig. 2 a-2b), the as-synthesized CDs was mono-dispersed in the range of 3-8 nm with the average diameter of about 4.96 nm. Though no high-resolution TEM images were obtained in this work to state the lattice spacing of CDs, the XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 2c) displayed a broad peak centered at about 2θ=22 °C, indicating a graphitic nature with highly disordered carbon atoms [47]. The FTIR was also performed to further analyze the composition of the as-synthesized CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 d, the broad absorption band at 3000-3500 cm−1 could be assigned to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, indicating that there were lots of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. While the bending vibrations of N-H groups and stretching vibrations of C=O groups appeared at 1566 cm−1 and 1655 cm−1 respectively, indicating that CDs was generated from the dehydration-condensation reaction between citric acid and DETA. In the UV-Vis spectra, a typical absorption peak at 336 nm was observed for the as-prepared CDs with a small shoulder band at 228 nm (orange line in Fig. 2e). The peak at 228 nm was consistent with the π-π* transition of C=C, resulting in nearly no obvious fluorescence emission [48]. Whereas the band-gap transition centered about 336 nm, due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states or n-π* transitions of C=O, could lead to strong fluorescence emission [49]. Subsequent research on fluorescence spectra manifested that the maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were around 370 nm and 454 nm, respectively (light and dark blue line in Fig. 2e). Moreover, no perceptible shift of the emission wavelength was observed in the excitation wavelength range of 360-400 nm (Fig. 2f), illustrating an excitation-independent emission of the as-prepared CDs. Therefore, the as-prepared CDs in aqueous solution, which emitted strong blue fluorescence under the illumination of 365 nm UV light, could meet the requirement for the construction of the IFE-based system for colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing of peroxides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism establishment of the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: As designed, the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system was constructed by combining the CDs aqueous solution with the TiOSO4-H2SO4 mixture which was prepared by mixing TiOSO4 and 98% H2SO4 with the volume ratio of 1:1. The H2SO4 not only inhibitted the hydrolysis of TiOSO4 but also assisted the colorimetric reaction between TiOSO4 and H2O2. Therefore, several measurements were employed to confirm the feasibility and mechanism of the designed sensing principle. As shown in Fig. 3a, the FL intensity at 454 nm of the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent sensing system consisting of CDs/TiOSO4 aqueous solution (blue curve) could be effectively quenched after the addition of 0.01 M hydrogen peroxide (black curve). Correspondingly, the blue fluorescence visible to naked eye of CDs became shallow with the exposure at a 365 nm UV lamp, confirming the fluorescence quenching of the CDs solution (inset, Fig. 3a). Meanwhile, the addition of hydrogen peroxide produced almost no significant fluorescence change in the CDs or titanyl sulfate solutions alone (Fig. S1a) and neglectable absorption change was observed in the CDs solution alone (Fig. S1b), indicating that both the CDs and titanyl sulfate aqueous solution were required for the colorimetric-fluorescent sensing of H2O2. From Fig. 3b, it could be seen that the absorption spectrum of titanyl sulfate with addition of H2O2 remarkably overlapped the fluorescence emission of CDs, illustrating that quenching mechanism was probably attributed to the IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The IFE-based mechanism was further experimentally confirmed. First, the introduction of increasing amounts of the reaction product of titanyl sulfate and H2O2 into CDs solution resulted in gradual fluorescence quenching in Fig. 3c. During the fluorescence quenching, the maximum emission wavelength of the CDs remained the same. Second, the fluorescence lifetime decay also added evidence for the IFE by giving the excited-state information of the fluorophore. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system was 1.90 ns, while the fluorescence lifetime after adding H2O2 was 1.97 ns with the fluctuation of less than 0.1 ns, which exhibitted no obvious change as shown in Fig. 3d. This result eliminated the FRET mechanism was invalid due to the no reduction of the fluorescence lifetime based on CDs . Therefore, the fluorescence quenching by H2O2 towards CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system should be ascribed to the IFE mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen peroxide sensing performance of the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The composition of the acidic CDs/TiOSO4 aqueous IFE-based sensing system was firstly optimized by comparing the fluorescent sensing performance with various composition ratio of CDs/TiOSO4-H2SO4 mixture towards the same amount of hydrogen peroxide. The fluorescence emission of the CDs/TiOSO4-H2SO4 mixture with various composition ratio in the range of 1.67 × 10−3-6.67 × 10−2 was quenched by hydrogen peroxide (1 mM) with different quenching efficiencies at 457 nm plotted in Fig. S2. It was obvious that with the increasing ratio of TiOSO4-H2SO4, the quenching efficiency of the sensing system increased first and then decreased. The highest quenching efficiency for 1 mM hydrogen peroxide of 36% was achieved at 0.00333% for the ratio of TiOSO4-H2SO4, which was then chosen as the optimized composition for the following sensing performance evaluation with hydrogen peroxide as standard target.

The fluorescent and colorimetric sensitivity of the acidic CDs/TiOSO4 aqueous IFE-based dual-mode sensing system towards hydrogen peroxide was evaluated in the concentration range of 0.0005 -50 mM and 0.2-10 mM respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 a and the inset, the fluorescence intensity of the emission centered at 457 nm decreased gradually with the increase of hydrogen peroxide concentration, accompanying with the blue fluorescence gradually visual darkening until it was completely quenched with the hydrogen peroxide concentration of 50 mM. It should be noted that the fluorescence intensity at 457 nm showed a linear behavior towards the increased concentration of hydrogen peroxide in the range of 5.0 × 10−4-1.0 mM with a correlation coefficient R2=0.986 (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, the LOD (defined as 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution and m is the slope of the linear fitting), for the CDs/TiOSO4 dual-mode sensing system towards hydrogen peroxide was calculated to be 0.2 μM. Meanwhile, with the increasing concentration of hydrogen peroxide, the absorption intensity of the dual-mode sensing system at 417 nm increased with color change from colorless to yellow (Fig. 4c and inset). Furthermore, the absorbance intensity at 417 nm increased linearly in the range of 0.02-10 mM, with a correlation coefficient R2=0.996, as shown in Fig. 4d. The colorimetric LOD was then calculated to be 31.3 μM. It was worthy-noting that the LOD of the colorimetric mode was two orders of magnitude higher than that of fluorescent mode, demonstrating that the IFE-based dual-mode sensing system was beneficial for the improvement of sensitivity.

The fluorescent and colorimetric selectivity of the acidic CDs/TiOSO4 aqueous IFE-based dual-mode sensing system towards hydrogen peroxide had been further investigated. A comparison of the fluorescent sensing performance towards hydrogen peroxide (1 mM) with various other redox compounds, explosive raw materials, and common substances with concentration of 1 mM, including sodium thiosulfate, sodium bisulfite, ammonium nitrate, potassium bisulfate, potassium carbonate, potassium iodide, urea, sodium hypochlorite, potassium nitrate, potassium chlorate, sodium perchlorate, and ammonium chloride, showed that they all failed to cause significant fluorescence quenching of the sensing system (Fig. 5 a). As summarized in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence quenching efficiency at 457 nm towards hydrogen peroxide was over 60%, while the quenching efficiencies for other substances were all less than 13%, indicating good fluorescent selectivity of the CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system for hydrogen peroxide. To further evaluate the selectivity of the CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system, likewise, a comparison of the colorimetric sensing performance for other redox compounds, explosive raw materials, and common substances, as for the fluorescent selectivity evaluation with extension to TNT, DNT, PNT, sulphur, sodium chloride, sodium bromide, and sodium sulphate, showed that they all failed to lead significant absorbance change for the sensing system (Fig. 5c). As summarized in Fig. 5d, the absorption intensity at 417 nm towards hydrogen peroxide was over 0.125, while the intensities for blank and other substances were all less than 0.03, demonstrating the good colorimetric selectivity of the CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system towards hydrogen peroxide, which was determined by the complexing reaction nature between the Titanium(IV) and peroxide. Therefore, the acidic CDs/TiOSO4 aqueous IFE-based dual-mode sensing system had been evidenced to possess relatively good fluorescent and colorimetric selectivity towards hydrogen peroxide among all these interferents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing platform for peroxide-based explosives
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs/TiOSO4 IFE-based dual-mode sensing system had been applied for the colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing towards a variety of peroxides, including raw materials for PBEs, such as 3% hydrogen peroxide as antibacterial liquid, sodium peroxide particulatesand calcium peroxide particulates, as well as the peroxide-based explosives, such as triacetone triperoxide (TATP) and hexamethylene triperoxide diamine (HMTD), as shown in Fig. 6 a. It was obvious that the CDs/TiOSO4 IFE-based dual-mode sensing system was capable to detect all the organic and inorganic peroxides with a dual-mode manner of color changing from colorless to yellow and blue fluorescent emission quenching with various extent, demonstrating the general applicability of the system for the sensing of peroxides.

Furthermore, the CDs/TiOSO4 IFE-based dual-mode sensing system was incorporated with a polyacrylamide (PAM) hydrogel, which was synthesized by applying acrylamide as monomer, N,N'-methylene bisacrylamide as crosslinker, ammonium persulfate as initiator, and N,N,N',N'-tetramethylethylenediamine as catalyst, to fabricate a hydrogel-based dual-mode sensing platform as illustrated in Fig. 6b. The SEM images of the PAM hydrogel before and after incorporation procedure for 24 hours showed that the hydrogel had the porous structure with pore size of 15-20 μm, which remained intact after incorporating the CDs/TiOSO4 IFE-based dual-mode sensing system (Fig. S3a and b), indicating its practicability as peroxide sensing platform. The EDS mapping results of sulphur and titanium elements showed that the CDs/TiOSO4 IFE-based dual-mode sensing system was uniformly dispersed in the hydrogel platform, as shown in Fig. S3c and S3d.

The sensing performance of the hydrogel-based dual-mode sensing platform was investigated. As shown in the inset of Fig. 6c, the fluorescent images of the hydrogel-based dual-mode sensing platform for sensing of hydrogen peroxide with different concentrations were captured under 365 nm UV excitation and analyzed in photoshop for the average RGB values. Then, the average G values of the images were plotted as a function of the concentration of hydrogen peroxide (Fig. 6c). It could be seen that with the concentration of hydrogen peroxide increasing to 10 mM, the blue fluorescent emission of the hydrogel gradually weakened, which was noticeable by naked eye observation with a B value decrease of 42, while with the concentration of hydrogen peroxide further increasing to 9.8 M, the blue fluorescent emission of the hydrogel was completely quenched observed by naked eye with a further B value decrease of 65. Moreover, the hydrogel sensing platform could also realize the fluorescent sensing of 3% hydrogen peroxide as liquid, and sodium peroxide and hexamethylene triperoxide diamine (HMTD) as particulates, indicating its feasibility in real scenario as visual sensor for both solid and liquid phase peroxides (Fig. S4).

Furthermore, the peroxide vapor sensing was realized by constructing an aerogel sensing platform by simply freeze drying the hydrogel-based dual-mode sensing platform. The vapor sensing performance was also evaluated by exposing the aerogel sensing platform in the hydrogen peroxide vapors with different concentrations (Fig. 6d and inset). The colorimetric sensing images showed that with the vapor concentration of hydrogen peroxide increasing to 16.6 ppm, the yellow color got darker to be observed. While in the fluorescent sensing mode, with the vapor concentration of hydrogen peroxide increasing to 221 ppm, the blue fluorescent emission of the hydrogel was weakened and finally quenched. As shown in Fig. 6d, the fluorescent intensity decreased sharply and nearly linearly within the vapor concentration range of 0-3.6 ppm, and decreased slowly with the vapor concentration further increasing to 221 ppm. The LOD was then calculated to be 0.15 ppb, which was much lower than the safety limit of 1.00 ppm [50]. Therefore, by incorporating with hydrogel substrate, the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system could realize the on-site visual detection of peroxides, including peroxide-based explosives, in liquid, solid and even vapor phases.

In summary, the CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system was developed for colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode visual sensing of peroxide-based explosives in liquid, solid and vapor phases. With addition of peroxides, the system exhibitted color change from colorless to yellow in colorimetric channel and blue emission quenching in fluorescent channel, endowing its dual-mode visual sensing capability for peroxides. Taking hydrogen peroxide as a representative, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched while the absorbance of (TiO2)2+ product was enhanced with the concentration of hydrogen peroxide from 0.0005 to 50 mM. The mechanism was validated as IFE through investigating the UV-vis absorption, emission spectra overlapping and fluorescence lifetime. The colorimetric and fluorescent LODs of the CDs/TiOSO4 sensing system were calculated to be 31.3 μM and 0.2 μM, respectively. The IFE-based dual-mode sensing system spontaneously owned the good sensitivity of fluorescent channel, which was 156 times higher than that of colorimetric channel, and relatively good fluorescent and colorimetric selectivity towards hydrogen peroxide, which was originated from the specific colorimetric reaction between TiOSO4 and peroxides. Moreover, incorporated with hydrogel, the IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system was applied to the on-site visual detection of liquid, solid and even vapor peroxides with vapor sensing LOD of 0.15 ppb. The present study provides valuable and universal design strategy for constructing sensitive and selective IFE-based colorimetric-fluorescent dual-mode sensing system for peroxides, which is inspirable for the future development of other IFE-based dual-mode sensors for various analytes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Xinjiang Key Laboratory for Explosives Safety Science (2018D04009), the Xinjiang Program of Introducing High Level Talents, and the Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences (CAS Grant No. ZDBS-LY-JSC029).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2021.10.002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Artificial olfactory system for trace identification of explosive vapors realized by optoelectronic schottky sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Guo, Z. Yang, X. Dou, Artificial olfactory system for trace identification of explosive vapors realized by optoelectronic schottky sensing, Adv Mater29 (2017) 1604528.
